hq: Not disclosed (no physical offices; operates from working space managed by building management)

system_description.overview: Confident AI, Inc. operates the Confident AI platform, a cloud-based LLM evaluation and safety platform. Companies of all sizes use Confident AI to benchmark, safeguard, and improve LLM applications with best-in-class metrics and guardrails. The company also maintains DeepEval, a widely-used open-source LLM evaluation framework.

products.Confident AI Platform: Cloud-based LLM evaluation platform; benchmark, safeguard, and improve LLM applications; best-in-class metrics and guardrails for AI systems
products.DeepEval: Open-source LLM evaluation framework; maintained separately from the cloud platform with an Open-Source Team

infrastructure.cloud_provider: AWS (primary, based on network diagram) + Supabase (named subservice organization, carve-out). Report text states 'hosted in Supabase in a virtual private cloud (VPC)' but network diagram on page 19 clearly shows AWS VPC with EKS cluster and RDS.
regions: Not specified by name; multi-availability zone deployment confirmed
infrastructure.physical_presence: None - no physical offices; operates from working space managed by building management
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (disaster recovery via 'spinning up multiple servers across all availability zones in Supabase')

network_architecture.network_segmentation: AWS VPC with network segmentation. System components configured such that company and customers' access is appropriately segmented from other customer accounts. Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality.
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management. Firewall access restricted to authorized personnel.
network_architecture.ids_ips: Intrusion detection system (IDS) utilized to monitor and detect unauthorized access or anomalies within the network, ensuring timely identification and response to potential security incidents. IDS/IPS deployed via Supabase per system description.
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via VPN/bastion host (referenced in pages 1-20 network description)
network_architecture.tls: Encryption technologies used to protect communication and transmission of data over public networks (TLS, version not specified)
network_architecture.topology: AWS VPC with Route Table. Private subnets contain EKS Cluster with EKS Nodes running Frontend UI Pods, API Server Pods, Online Evaluation Server Pods, and Application Load Balancer. RDS (PostgreSQL) in private subnets. Public subnets contain NAT Gateway. External connections: IdP Authentication Server, Company Network + VPN + DeepEval. Internet gateway at boundary.

application_architecture.pattern: Microservices (EKS cluster with separate pods for Frontend UI, API Server, Online Evaluation Server)
application_architecture.environments: At minimum: Production, Development/Test (separate environments confirmed by CC8.1.4 and CC8.2 controls)

databases: RDS (PostgreSQL) - visible in network diagram on page 19
data_storage.backup_frequency: Daily (CC7.4.2: 'Backups are taken daily, multi-availability zones are configured')

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Supabase (passwords controlled through 'Password policy of Supabase'); IdP Authentication Server shown in network diagram
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to production platform; MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3); MFA enforced for all remote access (CC6.1.1)
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Supabase password policy enforced: periodic forced changes, password expiry and complexity requirements, account lockout after limited failed attempts

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Git-based source code management tool (reference to 'master branch'; MFA required for access)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to require additional approval for every merge request moving change to production environment (master branch)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Configuration management tool used for baseline configurations, implementation, and rollback capability (tool not named)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO; monthly patch management reviews via ITLC meetings; workstations scanned for patch compliance daily; automatic OS updates configured

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named (monitoring tool generates alerts at predefined thresholds; infrastructure performance continuously monitored)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled for admin activities, logon attempts, data deletions, function changes, security configurations, permissions, and roles at application and infrastructure level
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts to IT management for high and critical risk events; change-management/ticketing application for tracking

third_party_services.purpose: Cloud infrastructure / database subservice organization - VPC, IDS/IPS, physical security of data center (carve-out)

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed on all production servers (continuous scanning, weekly AV definition updates) and Mac workstations/laptops
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications; automated tool implemented; management reviews high/critical findings
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party; high/critical findings remediated and tracked via incident management process

untestable: CC2.2.3 - No significant personnel changes during engagement period (control could not be tested)
CC3.2.1 (cybersecurity insurance) - No cyber incidents during engagement period
CC6.5.2 - No customer terminations during engagement period
CC7.3.1 - No security incidents reported during engagement period
excluded_criteria: Processing Integrity and Privacy trust services categories - Confident has determined these are not relevant to the system
CC6.4 - Physical access controls: Supabase responsible for physical security of data center; excluded from scope
A1.2 - Environmental protections/backup/recovery: Confident has no office/operates from working space managed by building management; all environmental security managed by Supabase
notable_controls: Risk and Governance Executive Committee (RGEC) established; meets semiannually to review key internal controls
IT Leadership Committee (ITLC) meets weekly to review security commitment activities
Quarterly access reviews for all user accounts including privileged accounts
Source code access restricted to authorized users with MFA (CC8.1.3)
Baseline configurations retained as code with configuration management tool for rollback
BCDR tested annually with different loss scenarios per defined testing schedule
EKS-based microservices architecture with separate pods for UI, API, and evaluation workloads

infrastructure.rationale: AWS VPC confirmed via network diagram with EKS cluster, RDS (PostgreSQL), Application Load Balancer, NAT Gateway - actual named services visible. Multi-AZ deployment and daily backups confirmed. Supabase as subservice org adds some ambiguity (report text vs. diagram shows AWS). No multi-region failover. CDN not mentioned. Strong infrastructure specificity compared to peers.

application_architecture.rationale: EKS-based microservices confirmed with separate pods for Frontend UI, API Server, and Online Evaluation Server - meaningful architectural detail. Application Load Balancer present. Separate environments confirmed. Branch protection and code review enforced. No programming languages or frameworks named. API style not disclosed.

data_layer.rationale: RDS (PostgreSQL) specifically named in network diagram. Daily backups with multi-AZ confirmed. Data classification policy, retention policy, and per-tenant segregation all confirmed. No caching layer or file storage mentioned. No data warehouse. Limited to one named database technology.

security_posture.rationale: Annual external pen test, monthly vulnerability scans, IDS/IPS via Supabase, firewalls, antivirus on all endpoints, MFA enforced for all remote access, KMS encryption at rest, cybersecurity insurance, quarterly access reviews. No WAF explicitly mentioned. No named SIEM. Strong security fundamentals for a company of this size.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection enforced, Kubernetes (EKS) confirmed, separate environments, emergency change process, monthly patch reviews with automatic patching, BCDR tested annually. CI/CD tool not named, IaC tool not specifically named. Kubernetes is a significant positive signal for deployment maturity.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually. Annual testing performed with multiple loss scenarios. Daily backups with multi-AZ. Multi-AZ failover by spinning up servers across all Supabase availability zones. No RTO/RPO disclosed. No multi-region failover. Business impact analysis referenced.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Only Supabase identified as a subservice organization. No other third-party vendors named despite the actual AWS VPC infrastructure visible in the network diagram. The report text conflates Supabase with the infrastructure but the diagram shows AWS. Single named vendor with no backup cloud provider disclosed.

overall.rationale: Confident AI demonstrates a solid security baseline with clean SOC 2 Type 2 opinion, no exceptions, annual pen test, monthly vuln scans, MFA across all access, EKS-based microservices, and quarterly access reviews. The network diagram revealing actual AWS VPC with EKS, RDS, and ALB is a meaningful positive signal for technical maturity. Key gap is the ambiguity between report text (says Supabase is the VPC) vs. the diagram (clearly shows AWS VPC with Kubernetes) - suggesting the Supabase layer may be a managed offering running on AWS. Report follows the same Accorp Partners template as other companies in this cohort with template-highlighted content remaining. No RTO/RPO disclosed, no named CI/CD tool or SIEM.

red_flags: Infrastructure description contradicts network diagram: report text says 'hosted in Supabase in a virtual private cloud (VPC)' but the network diagram on page 19 clearly shows AWS VPC with EKS cluster and RDS - the actual cloud provider relationship is ambiguous
Report contains highlighted yellow template instructions not fully removed (same Accorp Partners / Tenzo template pattern observed in other reports in this cohort)
No specific technology stack disclosed in narrative (no languages, frameworks, or CI/CD tools named)
yellow_flags: Only one subservice organization (Supabase) identified - actual AWS infrastructure not acknowledged as a separate subservice
No WAF mentioned despite operating a web-based SaaS/API platform
No monitoring tool or SIEM named specifically
3-month audit period (March 24 - June 24, 2025) is the minimum typical for SOC 2 Type 2 - likely a first Type 2 report
green_flags: Clean SOC 2 Type 2 opinion - no exceptions noted in any tested controls
Network diagram reveals actual Kubernetes (EKS) microservices architecture with named components - higher technical transparency than typical in this cohort
RDS (PostgreSQL) specifically identified as the production database
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party
MFA enforced across production platform, source code tools, and all remote access
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed
Access revocation within one business day of termination
BCDR tested annually with multiple loss scenarios
Management assertion properly signed by CTO Kritin Vongthongsri (unlike Company 1 which had blank signature)
Kubernetes container orchestration signals deployment maturity
key_observations: Confident AI is an LLM evaluation platform company that also maintains DeepEval, a widely-used open-source framework - dual product structure (commercial SaaS + open-source) is reflected in the org chart showing separate Open-Source Team and Platform Team under the CTO

The network diagram on page 19 is an actual infrastructure diagram (not a placeholder sample like Company 1) and reveals AWS VPC with EKS cluster, EKS Nodes running three separate pod types, RDS PostgreSQL, Application Load Balancer, and NAT Gateway - this is the most technically specific infrastructure disclosure observed so far in this cohort

The tension between 'hosted in Supabase in a VPC' (narrative text) and the AWS VPC diagram suggests Supabase may be used as the database/auth layer while AWS provides the primary compute infrastructure - a common architecture for AI startups
The ITLC meets weekly (vs. the RGEC semiannually) - same governance structure as Company 1, consistent with the Accorp Partners template

4 controls were untestable due to no triggering events (same pattern as Company 1): no personnel changes, no cyber incidents, no customer terminations, no security incidents - common in short-period first Type 2 reports

The DeepEval open-source project creates an interesting security boundary: the Platform Team builds the commercial product while the Open-Source Team maintains the public framework - any security issue in DeepEval could affect Confident AI's reputation and platform adoption
